sanskrit_devanagari:
अव्यक्तं व्यक्तिमापन्नं मन्यन्ते मामबुद्धय: ।
परं भावमजानन्तो ममाव्ययमनुत्तमम् ॥ २४ ॥
sanskrit_iast: avyaktaṁ vyaktim āpannaṁmanyante mām abuddhayaḥparaṁ bhāvam ajānantomamāvyayam anuttamam

translations.verbatim_quote: Unintelligent men, who do not know Me perfectly, think that I, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Kṛṣṇa, was impersonal before and have now assumed this personality. Due to their small knowledge, they do not know My higher nature, which is imperishable and supreme.

Unintelligent men, who do not know Me perfectly, think that I, the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Kṛṣṇa, was impersonal before and have now assumed this personality. Due to their small knowledge, they do not know My higher nature, which is imperishable and supreme.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 6036 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Those who are worshipers of demigods have been described as less intelligent persons, and here the impersonalists are similarly described. Lord Kṛṣṇa in His personal form is here speaking before Arjuna, and still, due to ignorance, impersonalists argue that the Supreme Lord ultimately has no form.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.24 Abuddhayah, the unintelligent, the non-discriminating ones; ajanantah, unaware; mama, of My; param, supreme; bhavam, state, My reality as the supreme Self; which is avyayam, immutable, undecaying; and anuttanam, unsurpassable; manyante, think; mam, of Me; as avyaktam, the unmanifest,
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-7-24-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The abuddhayaḥ (non-discriminating, ignorant) — unaware of Krishna's supreme state, immutable, unsurpassable — think Krishna, who is avyakta (the always-unmanifest), has now become manifest through embodiment. They are unaware of his ever-well-known reality.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.24 Ignorant people do not know My higher nature, immutable and unsurpassed. They do not know that it is I, who is worshipped through all rites, who is the Lord of all, and whose nature is beyond speech and mind, that has incarnated as the son of Vasudeva, without abandoning My divine nat
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-7-24-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ignorant people do not know Krishna's higher nature, immutable and unsurpassed — that Krishna, worshipped through all rites, Lord of all, has incarnated as the son of Vasudeva without abandoning his divine nature, out of compassion and to be the refuge of those who resort to him. They consider him only a worldly prince, manifest now where he was not before.

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All readings agree on the structural mistake: the unintelligent take a manifest appearance to be Krishna's full reality and miss the higher state (paraṁ bhāvam). Shankara reads avyaktam as the always-unmanifest brahman-state behind any apparent manifestation; Ramanuja reads avyaktam as the immutable divine nature that the incarnation does not abandon. The engineering analog is heavily STRETCHED — the verse claims metaphysical concealment behind apparent visibility, which is much larger than any operational analog.

literal_meaning: The unintelligent think me — the unmanifest — to have come into manifestation; not knowing my higher state, supreme, imperishable.
traditional_meaning_consensus: avyaktaṁ vyaktim āpannaṁ manyante mām abuddhayaḥ / paraṁ bhāvam ajānanto mamāvyayam anuttamam — the abuddhayaḥ (non-discriminating) think Krishna — who is avyakta (unmanifest) — to have come into manifestation (vyaktim āpannam); they do not know Krishna's higher state (paraṁ bhāvam), supreme (anuttamam), imperishable (avyayam). Shankara: they think Krishna has now become visible after being invisible; they do not know the supreme reality is beyond manifest/unmanifest distinctions. Ramanuja: ignorant people consider Krishna a worldly prince who was unmanifest before and is now manifest; they do not know the higher nature, immutable, the Lord of all, who has incarnated without abandoning his divine nature.

remaining_gaps: Madhva, Abhinavagupta, Sridhara Swami, Kesava Kashmiri commentaries available in raw/ but not pulled into the source pack.
